full_response:
Algorithmic idea  
1. Wrap the faulty device in a “reliable’’ comparison routine Reliable-Compare.  
   • To compare two tunnels x and y we call the device k independent times.  
   • We output the majority answer (break a tie by one extra call – this only helps).  
   • By choosing k = Θ(log n) we make a single Reliable-Compare wrong with
     probability at most 1/(200 n log n).  
2. Run any Θ(n log n)-comparison deterministic sorting algorithm, e.g. merge-sort,
   but replace every ordinary comparison by Reliable-Compare.

Because every ordinary comparison now costs Θ(log n) calls to the device, the
whole algorithm needs Θ(n log²n) calls.  With the above choice of k the union
bound shows that the probability that even one of the Θ(n log n) reliable
comparisons is wrong is < 0.01, hence the produced order is correct with
probability ≥ 0.99.

Below are the promised details.

------------------------------------------------------------
1   A reliable comparison

Let p = 1/4 be the error probability of one device call.  
Let q = 3/4 be the success probability.

Reliable-Compare(x, y):  
    k ← ⌈24 ln(200 n log₂ n)⌉     // make k odd  
    c ← 0  
    repeat k times:  
          if Device(x, y) says “x longer’’ then c ← c+1  
    if c > k/2 then return “x longer’’ else return “y longer’’

Error analysis (Chernoff).  
Let Xi ∼ Bernoulli(q) be 1 when the i-th call is correct.
X = ΣXi,  E[X] = μ = qk = ¾k.
Reliable-Compare is wrong exactly when X ≤ k/2.

Write k/2 = (1 − δ)μ :
          μ − k/2 = ¾k − ½k = ¼k
          δ = (¼k)/(¾k) = 1/3

Chernoff bound for the lower tail:

P[X ≤ (1−δ)μ] ≤ exp(−δ² μ /2) = exp(−(1/3)² · ¾k /2) = exp(−k/24)

Choose k as above; then

P[ Reliable-Compare wrong ] ≤ e^{−k/24}
                            ≤ e^{−ln(200 n log₂ n)}
                            = 1/(200 n log₂ n) .        (†)

------------------------------------------------------------
2   Sorting algorithm

Sort(A):                       // |A| = n distinct lengths
    if |A| ≤ 1 return A
    split A into A₁ and A₂ of equal size
    B₁ ← Sort(A₁)
    B₂ ← Sort(A₂)
    return Merge(B₁, B₂) using Reliable-Compare

The ordinary merge-sort proof shows that if every comparison
answers correctly then Sort returns A in increasing order.

------------------------------------------------------------
3   Cost

Merge-sort makes at most c n log₂ n ordinary comparisons
(for some constant c). Each ordinary comparison uses k =
Θ(log n) device calls, so

#device-calls = Θ(n log n · log n) = Θ(n log² n).

------------------------------------------------------------
4   Success probability

Let m ≤ c n log n be the number of Reliable-Compare calls.
By (†) each is wrong with probability ≤ 1/(200 n log n),
independently of the others.

By the union bound

P[some Reliable-Compare is wrong]
      ≤ m · 1/(200 n log n)
      ≤ c n log n · 1/(200 n log n)
      = c/200.

Take c ≤ 2 (true for merge-sort with binary comparisons), then
the failure probability is ≤ 0.01 and success probability ≥ 0.99.
For “sufficiently large’’ n the inequality (†) was derived
using ln(200 n log n) > 1; for small n we can simply increase k
by a constant factor.

------------------------------------------------------------
5   Space

Merge-sort needs Θ(n) extra space; Reliable-Compare uses O(1).

------------------------------------------------------------
6   Result

We have a Monte-Carlo algorithm using at most Θ(n log² n)
echolocation calls and producing the correct sorted order with
probability at least 0.99.

\boxed{\,\text{Wrap each comparison in }k=\Theta(\log n)\text{ majority
votes, then run merge-sort.  Total calls }=O(n\log^{2}n),\;
P[\text{sorted correctly}] \ge 0.99.\,}

Confidence: 0.24
